name: Angels Landing
highlights: The defining Zion hike — chain-assisted scramble to a rock fin suspended above the canyon
Walter's Wiggles: 21 tight switchbacks cut directly into the cliff, a feat of 1920s trail engineering
The final half-mile requires a permit (Angels Landing permit lottery — apply in advance at recreation.gov)
The view from the summit looking down both forks of Zion Canyon is unrepeatable
bookingWindow: Permit required for the final chain section — apply via recreation.gov lottery

name: The Narrows
location: Virgin River, Zion National Park — from Temple of Sinawava
highlights: Hiking in the Virgin River through a slot canyon up to 2,000 feet deep and 20 feet wide
The most distinctive hike in the American Southwest — no trail, the river is the trail
Neoprene socks, waterproof pants, and a walking stick are essential — rent from Zion Guru
The Narrows can close with little warning due to flash flood risk — always check conditions
bookingWindow: No permit for day hike from bottom; gear rental from Zion Guru recommended

name: Observation Point
location: East Mesa / Weeping Rock, Zion National Park
highlights: The highest viewpoint in Zion — Angels Landing is visible 1,000 feet below
No permit required (unlike Angels Landing) with equivalent or superior views
East Mesa approach (via the east entrance) is a gentler alternative to the Weeping Rock route
The secret local choice when Angels Landing permits are unavailable
bookingWindow: No permit required — early start recommended

name: Canyon Overlook Trail
location: Zion-Mount Carmel Highway, Zion National Park
highlights: The best short hike in Zion — exposed ledge trail above Pine Creek Canyon with full canyon views
No shuttle required — trailhead is on the Zion-Mount Carmel Highway just east of the tunnel
Morning light from this direction is exceptional — the canyon glows
Accessible for most fitness levels; some narrow ledge sections with drop-offs
bookingWindow: No permit required — roadside trailhead, limited parking

name: Zion Canyon E-Bike — Scenic Drive
location: Pa'rus Trail → Zion Canyon Scenic Drive → Temple of Sinawava
distance: 3.5 miles Pa'rus Trail + 6+ miles scenic drive
highlights: One of the great cycling experiences in any national park — the scenic drive is car-free beyond Zion Lodge, leaving only e-bikes and the occasional shuttle bus under 2,000-foot sandstone walls
Start on the Pa'rus Trail along the Virgin River, then turn onto the scenic drive and ride 6+ miles deep into the canyon at your own pace
Stop at every pull-out: Court of the Patriarchs, The Grotto, Weeping Rock, Temple of Sinawava — no shuttle schedule, no waiting
Early morning (7–10am) or late afternoon (2–5pm) for the best canyon light and thinnest crowds
bookingWindow: Book at zionguru.com — guided tours and self-guided rentals both available

name: Gooseberry Mesa — Mountain Biking
location: Hurricane, UT — 30 min from Zion's south entrance
highlights: One of the top mountain bike destinations in the Southwest — slickrock riding on a volcanic mesa above the Hurricane Valley
Multiple loop options from beginner-friendly to technical; the mesa rim trail offers Zion Canyon views to the north
BLM land — free, no permit required. Bring your own bike or rent in Hurricane.
bookingWindow: Self-guided — no booking. BLM land, free access.

name: Keyhole Canyon Canyoneering
location: Zion-Mount Carmel Highway, Zion National Park
highlights: The most accessible technical slot canyon in Zion — a single rappel (30 ft) followed by narrows swimming and stemming
Permit required (day-use canyoneering permit via recreation.gov) — limited entries per day
Wetsuit essential — the pools are cold. Full wetsuit and rappel gear available from Zion Guru.
A guided introduction to canyoneering for those new to the discipline — Zion Guru guided trips recommended
operator: Zion Guru (guided option or gear rental)
bookingWindow: Permit required at recreation.gov. Guided trips via zionguru.com.

name: The Subway — Advanced Canyoneering
highlights: The most celebrated slot canyon in Zion — a sculpted tube of Navajo sandstone carved by the Left Fork of North Creek
Multiple rappels, swims, and downclimbs through a corridor of perfect geological form
Permit required and highly competitive — apply 3 months in advance at recreation.gov
Technical route requiring prior canyoneering experience, wetsuit, and rappel gear — not for first-timers
bookingWindow: Permit required via recreation.gov lottery — highly competitive, apply 3 months ahead

name: Taylor Creek Trail — Kolob Canyons
location: Kolob Canyons district, Zion National Park — separate entrance off I-15, Exit 40
highlights: The canyon that most Zion visitors never see — Kolob Canyons has its own entrance, its own visitor center, and a fraction of the main canyon’s traffic
The trail follows the Middle Fork of Taylor Creek through two historic homestead cabins to Double Arch Alcove — a massive natural amphitheater
Multiple creek crossings (ankle-deep in spring, often dry by late summer) through a narrow canyon with cottonwood and Gambel oak
The load-spreader Zion needs — Kolob delivers canyon beauty without the shuttle, without the crowds, without the permit lottery
bookingWindow: Self-guided — no permit required. Free with Zion park pass. Kolob Canyons Visitor Center open daily.

name: Timber Creek Overlook — Kolob Canyons
location: End of Kolob Canyons Road, Zion National Park
highlights: The shortest trail in the guide delivers one of the biggest views — Kolob’s finger canyons, Pine Valley Mountains, and on clear days, the distant Mojave
Drive the 5-mile Kolob Canyons Scenic Road to the end, walk 10 minutes, and stand at the edge
Sunset here is extraordinary — the finger canyons glow red from a viewpoint you’ll likely have to yourself
Pair with Taylor Creek for a full Kolob half-day. You’ll wonder why you spent three days in the main canyon shuttle line.
bookingWindow: Self-guided — free with Zion park pass.

name: Kanarra Falls
location: Kanarraville, UT — 45 min from Zion’s south entrance via I-15
highlights: A slot canyon creek hike to a two-tier waterfall — you wade through knee-deep water between narrow red walls to reach the falls
Permit required ($15/person, 150 hikers per day) — the cap keeps the experience intimate. Book at kanarrafalls.com.
Two metal ladders bolted into the canyon wall provide access past the first falls to the upper canyon — the upper section is wilder and less visited
The Zion corridor’s best-kept open secret: slot canyon beauty at Narrows-level quality without the Narrows-level traffic
bookingWindow: Permit required — $15/person, book at kanarrafalls.com. 150/day cap. Weekends fill 1-2 weeks ahead.

name: Peek-a-Boo & Spooky Gulch — Grand Staircase-Escalante
location: Hole-in-the-Rock Road, Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument — 90 min from Zion via Mt. Carmel Junction
highlights: Two narrow slot canyons connected in a loop — Peek-a-Boo requires a 12-foot climb into the slot, Spooky narrows to shoulder-width in places
No permit required, no fee, no crowds (on weekdays). The most accessible slot canyon combo in southern Utah.
Hole-in-the-Rock Road is unpaved — passable in a passenger car when dry, impassable when wet. Check conditions.
For flexible or nomadic itineraries only — the 90-minute drive from Zion makes this a half-day commitment. Worth every mile.
bookingWindow: Self-guided — no permit required. Check Hole-in-the-Rock Road conditions before driving.

name: Cedar Breaks — Spectra Point Trail
location: Cedar Breaks National Monument — 60 min from Zion via Highway 14
highlights: A 10,350-foot natural amphitheater — like Bryce Canyon’s older, quieter sibling at twice the elevation with a tenth of the visitors
Spectra Point follows the rim through bristlecone pine forest (some trees 1,600+ years old) with views into the painted bowl of hoodoos below
Summer wildflower season (Jul-Aug) is extraordinary — alpine meadows of columbine, lupine, and Indian paintbrush at 10,000 feet
The elevation makes Cedar Breaks 20-30°F cooler than Zion — the ideal midday escape when the canyon floor is baking
bookingWindow: Self-guided — park entry fee applies. Scenic drive and Point Supreme viewpoint accessible without hiking.
